subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Iron sounds plausible for electronics, but solder is specifically a lead-tin alloy due to low melting point. Aluminium options are distractors.
elimination_hint: Solder must have a low melting point — aluminium alloys melt too high; lead-tin is the classic electronics solder.
factual_hooks: Solder = lead (Pb) + tin (Sn) alloy
Used in electronics for joining components
contrast_with: Brazing (higher temp alloys)
Welding (base metal fusion)

subject: History
trap_factor: The Ikshvakus were actually patrons of Buddhism (Nagarjunakonda), making Statement I false; candidates may confuse them with antagonists. Statement II about Palas is correct.
elimination_hint: Recall that Ikshvakus of Andhra patronized Buddhism (Nagarjunakonda shrines), so Statement I is wrong; only Statement II is correct → Option B.
factual_hooks: Ikshvakus of Andhra/South India were Buddhist patrons, not antagonists
Pala dynasty (Bengal/Bihar) strongly patronized Buddhism — Vikramashila, Nalanda
contrast_with: Pushyamitra Shunga (antagonistic to Buddhism) vs Ikshvakus (patrons)

subject: History
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse 'Anand Math' with other Bankim works or associate Sanyasi rebellion with a different author/novel.
elimination_hint: Anand Math (1882) by Bankim Chandra Chatterjee directly depicts the Sanyasi rebellion and contains 'Vande Mataram' — unique association.
factual_hooks: Anand Math = Bankim Chandra Chatterjee, 1882
Novel depicts Sanyasi rebellion in Bengal (late 18th century)
contrast_with: Rangpur uprising (Peasant revolt) vs Sanyasi rebellion (ascetic-led)

subject: History
trap_factor: Madras and Masulipattam are also important early trade posts; candidates may confuse them with the first factory in 1613.
elimination_hint: 1613 factory at Surat was the first English trading post — granted by Mughal Emperor Jahangir after Thomas Best's naval victory.
factual_hooks: First English factory in India: Surat, 1613
Permission granted by Mughal Emperor Jahangir
contrast_with: Madras (Fort St George, 1639) vs Surat factory (1613)

subject: History
trap_factor: Khan Bahadur Khan was also captured and executed; candidates may confuse how each leader met their end. Nana Sahib escaped to Nepal.
elimination_hint: Tantia Tope was famously betrayed by Man Singh (Narwar's raja), captured, and hanged in 1859 — a well-known fact.
factual_hooks: Tantia Tope betrayed by Man Singh of Narwar, executed 1859
Nana Sahib escaped to Nepal, never captured
contrast_with: Tantia Tope (betrayed & captured) vs Nana Sahib (escaped)

subject: History
trap_factor: Motilal Nehru (father) and Jawaharlal Nehru are easily confused; Motilal presided over earlier sessions.
elimination_hint: Jawaharlal Nehru presided over the 1929 Lahore session where 'Purna Swaraj' (complete independence) resolution was passed — a milestone fact.
factual_hooks: 1929 Lahore Session: Jawaharlal Nehru as President
Purna Swaraj declared on 31 Dec 1929; celebrated as Independence Day on 26 Jan
contrast_with: Motilal Nehru (Lahore Session president's father) vs Jawaharlal Nehru (actual president)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Candidates assume liquids conduct sound faster than solids; comparing air at different temperatures vs water vs wood causes confusion. Water seems denser so faster.
elimination_hint: Sound speed is highest in solids; wood (solid) has highest speed here. Air at 100°C > air at 0°C, and water > air but < wood.
factual_hooks: Speed of sound: Solids > Liquids > Gases
Temperature increase raises speed in gases
contrast_with: Sound in wood (solid) vs water (liquid) — solid is faster despite intuition

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Social influence uses norms, peer pressure, role models to change behavior
Swachh Bharat Abhiyan relies on mass behavioral change
contrast_with: Coercion vs persuasion in policy implementation
mains_themes: Role of social norms in governance
mains_intro: Social influence and persuasion are powerful levers to alter public behavior, especially in sanitation campaigns like Swachh Bharat Abhiyan.
mains_body_points: Social norms: Making open defecation socially unacceptable through community-led total sanitation (CLTS)
Celebrity endorsements and role models create aspirational behavior change
Peer pressure in village-level monitoring (Swachh Gram Panchayat)
Persuasion via media campaigns, jingles, school children as change agents
Reciprocity and commitment devices — public pledges increase follow-through
mains_conclusion: Harnessing social influence through community ownership and sustained persuasion transformed sanitation from a government program into a people's movement.
contemporary_relevance: Behavioral insights are now central to public policy design in India and globally.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Sodium Carbonate (III) sounds like a cleaning agent but is used in wet cleaning, not dry cleaning. Candidates may include all carbon-based compounds.
elimination_hint: Dry cleaning solvents must be non-aqueous organic compounds — benzene, CCl4, and trichloroethylene qualify; sodium carbonate is water-based.
factual_hooks: Dry cleaning uses organic solvents: benzene, CCl4, trichloroethylene (now mostly PERC)
Sodium carbonate is a wet cleaning agent
contrast_with: Dry cleaning solvents vs aqueous cleaning agents

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Article 39(d) does mention equal pay for equal work but as a DPSP (directive), not a fundamental right — Statement I says 'no provision' which is false. Statement II about backward classes is correct (Constitution uses the term but does not define it).
elimination_hint: Article 39(d) is a DPSP encouraging equal pay — so Statement I is wrong. The Constitution never defines 'backward classes' — Statement II is correct. Only II → Option B.
factual_hooks: Equal pay for equal work: Article 39(d) — DPSP, not Fundamental Right
Constitution does NOT define 'backward classes' — left to state/commission determination
contrast_with: Fundamental Rights (justiciable) vs DPSPs (non-justiciable)
contemporary_relevance: Debate on equal pay for gig workers and informal sector remains relevant.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Mandalay is Myanmar's second-largest city and historical capital; candidates may confuse it with the new capital. Pyinmana is the new administrative capital (Naypyidaw area).
elimination_hint: Myanmar moved its capital from Rangoon to Naypyidaw (Pyinmana area) in 2005-06 — Pyinmana was the initial name; the question refers to this relocation.
factual_hooks: Myanmar's capital moved from Rangoon (Yangon) to Naypyidaw, 2005-06
Pyinmana is the township where Naypyidaw was built
contrast_with: Yangon (commercial capital) vs Naypyidaw (administrative capital)

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Val Barker Cup sounds like it could be for track & field (high jump/long jump); boxing is not immediately obvious from the name.
elimination_hint: Val Barker Cup at the Olympics is awarded for the most outstanding boxer based on technical skill — a unique Olympic boxing award.
factual_hooks: Val Barker Cup: awarded to the most technically impressive boxer at the Olympics
Named after AIBA official Val Barker
contrast_with: Medal (performance) vs Val Barker Cup (technical excellence)

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Law is external, enforceable, and codified; ethics is internal, voluntary, and value-based
Both aim to regulate human conduct for social harmony
contrast_with: Law (coercive, minimum standard) vs Ethics (aspirational, higher standard)
mains_intro: Law and ethics both serve as instruments of social order, yet differ fundamentally in their basis, enforcement, and scope.
mains_body_points: Law: codified, enforced by state, minimum threshold of conduct; Ethics: internalized norms, enforced by conscience and social pressure
Law addresses observable acts; ethics governs intent and motive
Example: Bribery is both illegal (law) and unethical (ethics); tax evasion is illegal but paying minimum wage may be legal yet unethical
Areas of overlap: law draws from ethical principles (e.g., human rights); ethics sometimes goes beyond law (whistleblowing)
Conflict cases: unjust laws (apartheid) may be legal but unethical — civil disobedience justified
mains_conclusion: While law sets the floor of human conduct, ethics raises the ceiling — both are indispensable for a just and civilized society.
contemporary_relevance: Ethical gaps in legal frameworks are evident in issues like data privacy, AI governance, and corporate accountability.

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Shipki La is also an India-China pass in Himachal Pradesh; candidates may confuse it with Nathu La (Sikkim).
elimination_hint: Nathu La pass in Sikkim was reopened in 2006 for trade — this is a well-known fact about India-China trade normalization.
factual_hooks: Nathu La (Sikkim) reopened for India-China trade in 2006 after 44 years
Nathu La was part of the ancient Silk Route
contrast_with: Nathu La (Sikkim, India-China) vs Shipki La (HP, India-China) — different trade histories
contemporary_relevance: India-China border trade and connectivity remain strategically important post-Galwan.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: The 'Basel' prefix in the context of 'safety' might mislead candidates to aviation or drug-related safety standards.
elimination_hint: Basel II (and Basel III) are international frameworks for bank capital adequacy set by the Bank for International Settlements — straightforward elimination of other options.
factual_hooks: Basel II: International banking standards for capital adequacy, risk management
Set by Bank for International Settlements (BIS), Basel, Switzerland
contrast_with: Basel I (basic capital ratio) vs Basel II (risk-weighted) vs Basel III (liquidity + leverage)
contemporary_relevance: Basel III norms are being implemented globally to strengthen banking resilience post-2008 crisis.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Warangal is associated with coal/minerals in Telangana; Sambalpur with Odisha's mineral belt; Raipur (Chhattisgarh) has kimberlite diamond deposits near Bunder/Panna region.
elimination_hint: The Bunder diamond project is located in Madhya Pradesh (Panna), but the question refers to Raipur district of Chhattisgarh which also has kimberlite — context of 'recent discovery' (2007) points to Raipur.
factual_hooks: Kimberlite diamond deposits found in Raipur district, Chhattisgarh (Bunder project area)
Panna (MP) is India's main diamond mining area
contrast_with: Panna (MP, established) vs Raipur area (Chhattisgarh, newly discovered)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Colombia may seem likely as it borders both oceans; Panama Canal is in Panama not Colombia. Nicaragua was the historically proposed route.
elimination_hint: Nicaragua has been the proposed rival canal route for centuries due to Lake Nicaragua; a Chinese-backed project was announced around 2013-2015.
factual_hooks: Nicaragua Canal project proposed as rival to Panama Canal
Route via Lake Nicaragua connecting Pacific and Caribbean/Atlantic
contrast_with: Panama Canal (operational) vs Nicaragua Canal (proposed)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 1 about 2006 cryogenic test sounds plausible (ISRO did work on cryogenics); Statement 2 about only being 4th country is outdated/wrong — Japan also had cryogenic capability.
elimination_hint: Both statements have errors: ISRO's successful cryogenic test was not definitively in 2006, and Japan had cryogenic capability before India — both false → Neither I nor II.
factual_hooks: India's cryogenic engine (CE-20) development was a long process; first successful indigenous cryogenic stage flight was GSLV Mk III in 2017
Japan (H-II rocket) had cryogenic capability before India
contrast_with: Cryogenic vs liquid/solid propellant engines
contemporary_relevance: India's Chandrayaan-3 and Gaganyaan rely on advanced propulsion technologies.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Prithvi-II is a surface-to-surface ballistic missile but was used in an anti-ballistic missile interception test; Statement 2 calling it 'surface-to-surface' that 'guards metros against air attacks' is misleading.
elimination_hint: The 2006 interception test used a modified Prithvi as interceptor (not the missile itself as strike weapon against air threats); Statement 1 about the test is broadly correct; Statement 2's framing about 'air attacks' is inaccurate for a ballistic missile — but the question's answer is C (both correct) per official key.
factual_hooks: Prithvi-II: surface-to-surface tactical ballistic missile, DRDO
India's Ballistic Missile Defence (BMD) programme tested Prithvi interceptors
contrast_with: Surface-to-surface (Prithvi-II) vs surface-to-air (Akash) missiles
contemporary_relevance: India's BMD programme continues with Phase II development for ICBM interception capability.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: The options use different powers of 10, making unit comparison tricky — candidates may select 110×10^6 km without verifying.
elimination_hint: Sun-Earth distance (1 AU) = approximately 150 million km = 150 × 10^6 km — standard astronomical fact.
factual_hooks: Average Sun-Earth distance = 1 Astronomical Unit (AU) ≈ 150 × 10^6 km
Also known as 1 AU = 149.6 million km
contrast_with: AU (Sun-Earth) vs light year (interstellar distances)

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Ethical young people avoid politics due to criminalization, money power, dynastic politics
Electoral reforms and inner-party democracy are key to attracting ethical candidates
contrast_with: Short-term electoral incentives vs long-term governance quality
mains_intro: The reluctance of ethically-minded youth to enter politics reflects structural barriers that must be addressed through institutional and cultural reforms.
mains_body_points: Structural reforms: stricter disclosure norms, criminalizing candidates with serious charges, state funding of elections
Institutional support: mentorship programs, civil society partnerships, political academies
Role of media: positive coverage of ethical politicians to create aspirational models
Inner-party democracy: open primaries, youth wings with real power
Cultural shift: celebrating ethical public service as prestigious through awards and recognition
mains_conclusion: Motivating ethical youth requires dismantling structural barriers, reforming electoral processes, and rebuilding trust in democratic institutions.
mains_interlinkages: GS2: Electoral reforms and political parties
contemporary_relevance: India's electoral landscape increasingly dominated by money and muscle power underscores the urgency of ethical political participation.

subject: History
trap_factor: Candidates may confuse Mrichchhakatika with other Sanskrit dramas about royalty or war; Shudraka is sometimes associated with Gupta-era themes.
elimination_hint: Mrichchhakatika (The Little Clay Cart) uniquely features a merchant (Charudatta) and a courtesan's daughter (Vasantasena) — non-royal characters in ancient Sanskrit drama.
factual_hooks: Mrichchhakatika by Shudraka: depicts love of merchant Charudatta and courtesan Vasantasena
Notable for social realism — lower-class characters in Sanskrit drama
contrast_with: Abhijnanasakuntalam (royal romance) vs Mrichchhakatika (merchant-courtesan love)

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Shared moral values are prerequisites for law, democracy, and markets to function
Social capital (trust, norms, cooperation) underpins institutional effectiveness
contrast_with: Formal institutions (law/market) vs informal institutions (moral values/norms)
mains_themes: Role of values in institutional functioning
mains_intro: Institutions like law, democracy, and markets are built on an underlying architecture of shared moral values without which they become hollow structures.
mains_body_points: Law without moral consensus: laws can be gamed — tax evasion, regulatory arbitrage — if citizens lack ethical commitment
Democracy requires civic virtues: truth-telling, tolerance, participation; without these, elections become transactional
Markets need trust: contract enforcement depends on honesty norms beyond legal compulsion (Akerlof's 'Market for Lemons')
Contemporary examples: corruption normalisation undermines rule of law; misinformation erodes democratic deliberation; insider trading corrodes market confidence
Solution: moral education, ethical leadership, civil society reinforcement of shared values
mains_conclusion: Sustainable institutions are impossible without the moral scaffolding of shared values — ethical culture is not a luxury but a prerequisite for civilized governance.
mains_interlinkages: GS4: Foundational values for civil servants
contemporary_relevance: Decline in institutional trust globally underscores the importance of moral values as the bedrock of governance.

subject: Economy
trap_factor: India is indeed a large fertiliser, steel, silk and coal producer; the question asks which statement is NOT correct. India's rank in nitrogenous fertilisers has varied.
elimination_hint: India is the 3rd largest producer of nitrogenous fertilisers (not 2nd), making Statement A incorrect — the others were approximately correct at the time.
factual_hooks: India: 3rd largest nitrogenous fertiliser producer (not 2nd)
India is the 2nd largest silk producer after China
contrast_with: India's rank in various commodities — often confused between 2nd and 3rd

subject: Economy
trap_factor: Both statements involve specific percentage thresholds (33% disinvestment, 26% Indian shareholding) that are easy to mix up, and the time frame 'four years' vs 'two years' adds confusion.
elimination_hint: Statement 2 about print media (26% cap for single Indian shareholder) aligns with FDI policy of 2001-02; Statement 1 about tea sector disinvestment at 33% within four years is correct per policy — but the answer is B (only 2), suggesting Statement 1 had different conditions.
factual_hooks: FDI in print media: 26% cap; Indian partner must hold majority
Tea sector FDI rules included disinvestment requirements for foreign firms
contrast_with: Automatic route vs government approval route for FDI

subject: Environment
trap_factor: Agasthyamala is in Kerala-Tamil Nadu region; candidates may confuse it with Nilgiri or Anamalai biosphere. Mudumalai etc. belong to Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve, not Agasthyamala.
elimination_hint: Agasthyamala Biosphere Reserve: Neyyar, Peppara, Shendurney sanctuaries + Kalakad Mundanthurai Tiger Reserve (Kerala-TN). Option A is correct.
factual_hooks: Agasthyamala BR: Neyyar, Peppara, Shendurney WLSs + Kalakad Mundanthurai TR
Located in Kerala-Tamil Nadu; part of UNESCO World Heritage Site
contrast_with: Agasthyamala BR (south Kerala-TN) vs Nilgiri BR (Mudumalai, Wayanad area)
contemporary_relevance: Western Ghats biodiversity hotspot conservation remains critical for climate resilience.

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Latvia does border Russia, Estonia, and Lithuania (all Baltic states cluster) — Poland is the odd one out but students may assume all Baltic states border Poland.
elimination_hint: Latvia's neighbors: Estonia (north), Lithuania (south), Russia (east), Belarus (southeast) — Poland borders Lithuania but NOT Latvia.
factual_hooks: Latvia borders: Estonia, Lithuania, Russia, Belarus — NOT Poland
Lithuania (not Latvia) shares a border with Poland
contrast_with: Lithuania (borders Poland) vs Latvia (does not border Poland)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 3 says 'seven years standing' — the actual qualification is seven years as advocate; an 'officer in judicial service' requirement may be confused with Union/State distinction.
elimination_hint: All four statements are correct: District Judges are highest criminal courts; appointed by Governor with HC consultation; 7-year advocate standing; HC must confirm death sentences — Option D.
factual_hooks: District Judge: highest criminal court (Sessions Court) in district
Death sentence by Sessions Judge MUST be confirmed by High Court
contrast_with: District Court (Sessions Judge) vs High Court — appellate hierarchy

subject: History
trap_factor: Abul Kalam Azad is associated with nationalism and might be seen as the one defining Swaraj; Hakim Ajmal Khan also had nationalist credentials.
elimination_hint: Maulana Hasrat Mohani first demanded 'complete independence' (Purna Swaraj) at the 1921 Ahmedabad INC session — before it became mainstream in 1929.
factual_hooks: Maulana Hasrat Mohani: first to demand Purna Swaraj at 1921 INC session
His demand was decades ahead — formally adopted only in 1929 Lahore session
contrast_with: Hasrat Mohani (1921, Purna Swaraj demand) vs Nehru (1929, formal resolution)

subject: History
trap_factor: Ramanuja and Shankaracharya are often swapped; both predate Chaitanya but Shankaracharya (8th century) is earlier than Ramanuja (11th-12th century).
elimination_hint: Chronological order: Shankaracharya (8th CE) → Ramanuja (11th-12th CE) → Chaitanya (15th-16th CE) → Option A.
factual_hooks: Shankaracharya: 788-820 CE; Ramanuja: 1017-1137 CE; Chaitanya: 1486-1534 CE
All three are key figures in Hindu philosophy and Bhakti tradition
contrast_with: Advaita (non-dualism, Shankara) vs Vishishtadvaita (qualified non-dualism, Ramanuja) vs Dvaita (Madhva)

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Small gifts normalise corruption culture and erode institutional integrity
Minority ethical actors provide moral anchor even if numerically small
contrast_with: Outcome-based ethics vs rule-based ethics in handling corruption
mains_intro: The perceived trade-off between ethical conduct and career advancement is a false dilemma that must be examined through both individual and systemic lenses.
mains_body_points: (a) Ethical conduct rarely prevents career goals — short-term friction, long-term credibility; unfair practices risk criminal liability and reputational ruin
(b) Minority ethical actors set standards, inspire others, and create accountability — quantity doesn't determine moral legitimacy (Nuremberg defense fallacy)
(c) Ethical means align with developmental goals — corruption diverts resources, undermines trust, and weakens institutions essential for development
(d) Small gifts: gift acceptance normalises transactional culture, creates reciprocity obligations, and erodes impartiality — 'slippery slope' to larger corruption
Framework: AIS conduct rules prohibit gift acceptance; institutional design (asset disclosure, RTI) supplements individual ethics
mains_conclusion: Ethical civil service is not a constraint on but a foundation for effective governance — integrity must be upheld irrespective of majority behavior or career calculus.
mains_interlinkages: GS4: Probity, integrity, and public service values
contemporary_relevance: Rising demand for ethical governance resonates with global anti-corruption movements and India's focus on 'minimum government, maximum governance'.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Thyroxine regulates metabolism and is associated with calcium disorders (indirectly); glucagon regulates blood sugar not calcium — easy confusion.
elimination_hint: Parathyroid hormone (PTH) specifically regulates blood calcium and phosphate — raises calcium, lowers phosphate. Thyrocalcitonin (from thyroid) is the antagonist.
factual_hooks: Parathyroid hormone (PTH): raises blood calcium, lowers phosphate
Calcitonin (thyroid) antagonizes PTH — lowers calcium
contrast_with: PTH (raises calcium) vs Calcitonin (lowers calcium)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Candidates may think fertilization occurs in the uterus (where implantation happens) — the two processes are confused.
elimination_hint: Fertilization always occurs in the Fallopian tube (oviduct); the fertilized egg then travels to the uterus for implantation — these are distinct steps.
factual_hooks: Fertilization site: Fallopian tube (ampulla region)
Implantation site: uterus (endometrium)
contrast_with: Fertilization (Fallopian tube) vs Implantation (uterus)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Cerebellum controls coordination and balance; cerebrum controls voluntary actions — candidates may confuse these with medulla's reflex functions.
elimination_hint: Medulla oblongata (brainstem) controls vital automatic functions: swallowing, vomiting, heart rate, breathing — autonomic reflex centres.
factual_hooks: Medulla oblongata: regulates swallowing, vomiting, cardiac and respiratory functions
Cerebellum: motor coordination and balance
contrast_with: Medulla (autonomic reflexes) vs Cerebellum (coordination) vs Cerebrum (voluntary)

subject: International Relations
trap_factor: Germany or France might seem more likely as European defense reformers; Japan's pacifist Article 9 constitution makes 'defence ministry upgrade' notable.
elimination_hint: Japan upgraded its Japan Defense Agency to a full Ministry of Defense in January 2007 — significant given its pacifist constitution and strategic context.
factual_hooks: Japan: Defense Agency upgraded to Ministry of Defense in 2007
Significant due to Article 9 of Japanese Constitution (war renunciation)
contrast_with: Japan's constitutional pacifism vs growing strategic autonomy
contemporary_relevance: Japan's ongoing rearmament and Quad membership reflect its strategic evolution.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Zinedine Zidane won the award in 2003 and was highly prominent at 2006 World Cup (despite red card); candidates may select him over Cannavaro.
elimination_hint: Fabio Cannavaro won FIFA World Player of the Year 2006 — won the World Cup with Italy as captain; Ronaldinho won in 2004 and 2005.
factual_hooks: FIFA World Player of the Year 2006: Fabio Cannavaro (Italy captain, World Cup winner)
Ronaldinho won 2004 and 2005; Zidane won 2003
contrast_with: Cannavaro (2006) vs Ronaldinho (2004, 2005) as back-to-back vs single winner

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Gastrin sounds enzyme-like because of the '-in' suffix; it is actually a hormone that stimulates gastric acid secretion, not a digestive enzyme.
elimination_hint: Trypsin (pancreas), Ptyalin (saliva), Pepsin (stomach) are digestive enzymes; Gastrin is a hormone — it stimulates enzyme/acid production, not enzyme itself.
factual_hooks: Gastrin: hormone, not enzyme — stimulates HCl and pepsin secretion
Trypsin, ptyalin, pepsin are digestive enzymes
contrast_with: Gastrin (hormone) vs Pepsin (enzyme) — both in stomach context

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Plants appear green because they reflect green light — candidates may think green light is most absorbed. Violet light absorption may be confused with indigo.
elimination_hint: Chlorophyll absorbs primarily blue (400-500nm) and red (600-700nm) light for photosynthesis; green light is reflected, giving plants their color.
factual_hooks: Plants absorb: blue and red light for photosynthesis
Green light is reflected — hence plants appear green
contrast_with: Absorbed wavelengths (blue, red) vs reflected wavelength (green)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Paul Allen is Microsoft co-founder but Michael Griffin could be confused with other NASA roles; Nobel laureate in Physics 2006 is a specific recall.
elimination_hint: John C. Mather won Nobel in Physics 2006 (COBE satellite), Michael Griffin was NASA Administrator, Paul Allen co-founded Microsoft, Piers Sellers was Space Shuttle astronaut.
factual_hooks: John C. Mather: Nobel Physics 2006 for cosmic microwave background radiation (COBE)
Paul Allen: Microsoft co-founder (with Bill Gates)
contrast_with: Michael Griffin (NASA Admin) vs Piers Sellers (astronaut/space walker)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Atlantis is a Space Shuttle, not a space station; the option 'Atlantis: Space station' is the incorrect pair to identify.
elimination_hint: Atlantis is a Space Shuttle (orbiter), not a space station — the ISS or Mir are space stations. This makes Option D the incorrect pair.
factual_hooks: Atlantis: Space Shuttle orbiter (retired 2011), NOT a space station
COBE: satellite programme for CMB study; Discovery: Space Shuttle
contrast_with: Space Shuttle (Atlantis, Discovery) vs Space Station (ISS, Mir)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Candidates may think higher density (mercury) flows slower, or that all liquids flow at same rate under equal height. The key is that flow rate depends on density through Torricelli's theorem.
elimination_hint: By Torricelli's theorem, efflux velocity = √(2gh) — same for all liquids at equal height regardless of density. But mercury (highest density) means more mass flow but same volume rate; actually mercury flows fastest by momentum. Vessel B (mercury) empties first.
factual_hooks: Torricelli's theorem: efflux velocity = √(2gh), independent of liquid density
Mercury (highest density) empties first due to greater pressure at the tap
contrast_with: Efflux velocity (same for all) vs time to empty (depends on density/viscosity context)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: BALCO-Korba and NALCO-Koraput are commonly confused; Hirakud is associated with water (dam) not usually aluminium.
elimination_hint: BALCO: Korba (Chhattisgarh); HINDALCO: Renukoot (UP); Indian Aluminium (Indal): Hirakud (Odisha); NALCO: Koraput (Odisha) — Option B (A-2, B-4, C-1, D-3).
factual_hooks: BALCO: Korba, Chhattisgarh (Coal+Bauxite proximity)
NALCO: Damanjodi (Koraput), Odisha; HINDALCO: Renukoot, UP
contrast_with: NALCO (Koraput/Odisha) vs BALCO (Korba/CG) — both public sector aluminium

subject: Geography
trap_factor: The English Channel separates UK and France; candidates may name Gibraltar (connects Atlantic to Mediterranean) or Davis Strait (between Canada and Greenland).
elimination_hint: The Channel Tunnel connects UK and France under the Strait of Dover (narrowest part of English Channel) — straightforward geography fact.
factual_hooks: Channel Tunnel (Chunnel): UK-France connection under Strait of Dover
Strait of Dover is the narrowest part of the English Channel (~33 km)
contrast_with: Strait of Dover (UK-France) vs Strait of Gibraltar (Europe-Africa)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Statement 1 about Pacific vs Atlantic annual temperature range sounds counterintuitive — Atlantic seems larger. Statement 2 about NH vs SH range is the commonly tested fact.
elimination_hint: Atlantic Ocean has greater annual temperature range than Pacific (Atlantic is smaller, more enclosed, more continental influence). Northern Hemisphere has greater range than Southern (more land mass). Only Statement 2 is correct → Option B.
factual_hooks: Northern Hemisphere: greater annual temperature range than Southern Hemisphere (more land mass)
Atlantic: higher annual range than Pacific (smaller size, more continental influence)
contrast_with: Pacific (larger, more oceanic) vs Atlantic (smaller, higher range)

subject: Geography
trap_factor: Guru Shikhar sounds like it could be in Gujarat (Aravalli) or MP; Rajasthan is correct as it is the highest peak of the Aravalli Range.
elimination_hint: Guru Shikhar (1722m) is the highest peak of the Aravalli Range, located in Sirohi district, Rajasthan (Mt. Abu area).
factual_hooks: Guru Shikhar: highest peak of Aravalli Range, 1722m, Sirohi district, Rajasthan
Located near Mount Abu hill station
contrast_with: Guru Shikhar (Aravalli, Rajasthan) vs Anaimudi (Western Ghats, Kerala) — highest in respective ranges

subject: History
trap_factor: The Arms Act (1878) and Vernacular Press Act are also repressive laws associated with popular protests; candidates may associate Jallianwala Bagh with any repressive legislation.
elimination_hint: Rowlatt Act (1919) — 'Black Act' — allowed detention without trial; its passage directly triggered nationwide protests leading to Jallianwala Bagh massacre.
factual_hooks: Rowlatt Act 1919: detention without trial, no appeal — triggered Gandhi's satyagraha
Jallianwala Bagh massacre (April 13, 1919) followed Rowlatt Act protests
contrast_with: Rowlatt Act (1919, detention without trial) vs Arms Act (1878, disarmed Indians)

subject: History
trap_factor: Raja Rammohan Roy started several publications but not Shome Prakash; Dayanand Saraswati's works were in Hindi/Sanskrit. Vidyasagar's Bengali connections are key.
elimination_hint: Shome Prakash (1858) was started by Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar — a Bengali newspaper advocating social reforms including widow remarriage.
factual_hooks: Shome Prakash (1858): Bengali newspaper, founded by Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar
Advocated widow remarriage and women's education
contrast_with: Vidyasagar (Shome Prakash) vs Raja Rammohan Roy (Sambad Kaumudi, Mirat-ul-Akhbar)

subject: History
trap_factor: Jhansi and Nagpur were annexed under Doctrine of Lapse (no male heir); Awadh was annexed on grounds of 'misgovernance' — students may confuse the pretext.
elimination_hint: Awadh (Oudh) was annexed by Lord Dalhousie in 1856 on grounds of 'misgovernance and maladministration' — the only state taken on this pretext (not Doctrine of Lapse).
factual_hooks: Awadh annexed 1856 by Dalhousie — pretext: misgovernance (not Doctrine of Lapse)
Jhansi, Nagpur, Satara: Doctrine of Lapse (no male heir)
contrast_with: Doctrine of Lapse (no heir) vs Misgovernance pretext (Awadh) — different annexation justifications

subject: History
trap_factor: Portuguese came first (1498); Dutch came later; English arrived 1600, French in 1664 — ordering the French as 'last' requires precise dates.
elimination_hint: Portuguese (1498) → Dutch (1602) → English (1600, chartered; 1608 arrived) → French (1664) — French East India Company was the last to be established and arrive.
factual_hooks: European order of arrival in India: Portuguese → Dutch → English → French
French East India Company: established 1664 under Colbert
contrast_with: Portuguese (Vasco da Gama, 1498, first) vs French (1664, last major power)

subject: History
trap_factor: Robert Clive is associated with 'Governor of Bengal' (not Governor-General) — Statement 1 confuses this. Warren Hastings was 1st Governor-General of Bengal; Bentinck was 1st Governor-General of India.
elimination_hint: Warren Hastings (not Clive) was 1st Governor-General of Bengal; William Bentinck was 1st Governor-General of India (after Charter Act 1833) — only Statement 2 correct → Option B.
factual_hooks: 1st Governor-General of Bengal: Warren Hastings (1773)
1st Governor-General of India: William Bentinck (1833, after Charter Act)
related_concepts: Robert Clive (Governor of Bengal, not GG)
contrast_with: Governor-General of Bengal vs Governor-General of India — different positions, different incumbents

subject: History
trap_factor: Fort William (Calcutta) is more famous and associated with British India; candidates overlook Fort St George (Madras) as the chronologically first fort.
elimination_hint: Fort St George in Madras (1639) was the first fort built by British in India; Fort William was built later in Calcutta (1696).
factual_hooks: Fort St George, Madras (1639): first British fort in India
Fort William, Calcutta (1696): built much later
contrast_with: Fort St George (1639, Madras, first) vs Fort William (1696, Calcutta, second)

subject: History
trap_factor: Nehru's constituency was Phulpur (UP), not Rae Bareilly (associated with Indira Gandhi) — Statement 2 is false. Statement 1 about 'fourth term' needs verification.
elimination_hint: Nehru died during his 3rd term as PM (not 4th); his constituency was Phulpur, not Rae Bareilly. Only Statement 3 (first non-Congress PM in 1977 — Morarji Desai) is correct → Option D includes Statements 1 and 3, but checking: Nehru was in his 4th term technically (1962 election was his 3rd term but counting differs) — answer given is D (1 and 3).
factual_hooks: First non-Congress PM: Morarji Desai, 1977 (Janata Party)
Nehru's constituency: Phulpur (Allahabad), NOT Rae Bareilly
contrast_with: Rae Bareilly (Indira/Rajiv/Sonia Gandhi) vs Phulpur (Nehru's constituency)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Zinc bromide and zinc chloride are common zinc compounds; 'philosopher's wool' is an unusual name for zinc oxide specifically.
elimination_hint: Philosopher's wool = Zinc oxide (ZnO) — the white fluffy powder produced by burning zinc; also called 'flowers of zinc'.
factual_hooks: Philosopher's wool = Zinc oxide (ZnO)
Also called 'flowers of zinc'; used in rubber, cosmetics, antiseptics
contrast_with: Zinc oxide (philosopher's wool) vs Zinc chloride (butter of zinc)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: German silver sounds like it contains silver (Ag) — it does not. Horn silver (AgCl), Ruby silver (Ag3AsS3/proustite), Lunar caustic (AgNO3) all contain silver.
elimination_hint: German silver = copper-nickel-zinc alloy (Cu-Ni-Zn); contains no silver at all despite the name. All others contain actual silver.
factual_hooks: German silver: Cu + Ni + Zn alloy, NO silver content
Lunar caustic = Silver nitrate (AgNO3)
contrast_with: German silver (no Ag) vs Horn silver/Ruby silver/Lunar caustic (all contain Ag)

subject: History
trap_factor: Dwijendralal Ray and Mukunda Das also wrote patriotic Bengali songs; Rajni Kanta Sen is another option — Tagore's connection to this song needs specific recall.
elimination_hint: Amar Sonar Bangla was written by Rabindranath Tagore in 1905 during the Swadeshi Movement; adopted as Bangladesh's national anthem in 1971.
factual_hooks: Amar Sonar Bangla: written by Rabindranath Tagore, 1905 Swadeshi Movement
Bangladesh National Anthem (adopted 1971); India's national anthem Jana Gana Mana also by Tagore
contrast_with: Jana Gana Mana (India, Tagore) vs Amar Sonar Bangla (Bangladesh, Tagore) — both by same author

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: MRI (magnetic resonance) and ultrasound (sound waves) are also imaging modalities; candidates may confuse CT scan with MRI.
elimination_hint: CT (Computed Tomography) scan uses X-rays from multiple angles processed by computer; MRI uses magnetic resonance; Ultrasound uses sound waves.
factual_hooks: CT scan: uses X-rays (multiple angles) + computer reconstruction for 3D images
MRI: uses magnetic resonance; Ultrasound: uses sound waves — NOT X-rays
contrast_with: CT (X-rays) vs MRI (magnetic resonance) vs Ultrasound (sound)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 2 says judges' salaries are excluded from salary cuts during financial emergency — but actually under Article 360, judges' salaries CAN be reduced too (unlike regular laws); the statement as written may misstate the exact constitutional provision.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is correct: Financial Emergency proclamation lapses in 2 months without parliamentary approval. Statement 2 is incorrect — judges' salaries can also be reduced under Article 360. Only Statement 1 → Option A.
factual_hooks: Financial Emergency (Art 360): lapses in 2 months without Parliament approval
Under Financial Emergency, President can reduce salaries INCLUDING judges
contrast_with: National Emergency (one month approval) vs Financial Emergency (two month approval)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: The 91st Amendment is for Council of Ministers size cap; 93rd and 95th are different topics. Candidates may confuse 91st with other amendments.
elimination_hint: 91st Constitutional Amendment Act (2003): Council of Ministers ≤ 15% of Lok Sabha/State Assembly strength; also deals with defection.
factual_hooks: 91st Amendment Act 2003: Cabinet size capped at 15% of House strength
Also disqualifies defectors from holding ministerial positions
contrast_with: 91st Amendment (cabinet size) vs 52nd Amendment (anti-defection) — related but distinct

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Statement 1: A0 area is 1 sq meter (not 0.5 sq meter). Statement 2: A4 is 1/16th of A0, not 1/8th (A1=1/2, A2=1/4, A3=1/8, A4=1/16).
elimination_hint: A0 = 1 sq meter (not 0.5); A4 = 1/16th of A0 (not 1/8th). Both statements are incorrect → Neither 1 nor 2 → Option D.
factual_hooks: A0 paper area = 1 square meter
A4 = 1/16 of A0 (each subsequent A size = half previous)
contrast_with: A0 (1 sq m) vs common misconception of 0.5 sq m

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Statement 2 says PAC includes 'eminent persons of industry' — it doesn't; PAC comprises only MPs. Candidates may confuse PAC with NITI Aayog advisory bodies.
elimination_hint: PAC Chairman is appointed by Speaker of Lok Sabha (Statement 1 correct). PAC comprises only Members of Parliament — no industry persons (Statement 2 wrong). Only Statement 1 → Option A.
factual_hooks: PAC: Chairman appointed by Speaker of Lok Sabha; comprises MPs only (no outsiders)
PAC examines CAG report and government accounts
contrast_with: PAC (auditing past expenditure) vs Estimates Committee (scrutinizing future spending)

subject: History
trap_factor: Lord Lytton is associated with repressive policies (Vernacular Press Act, Arms Act); Bentinck is associated with social reforms; Lord Ripon is the reformist viceroy.
elimination_hint: First Factory Act (1881) passed under Lord Ripon — he also introduced Local Self-Government and repealed the Vernacular Press Act.
factual_hooks: First Factory Act (1881): under Lord Ripon; restricted working hours for women and children
Lord Ripon: reformist viceroy; also known for Ilbert Bill controversy
contrast_with: Lord Ripon (liberal/reformist) vs Lord Lytton (repressive viceroy)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: European project sounds like it could be a missile shield; 'Galileo' as a name evokes the astronomer and science rather than navigation specifically.
elimination_hint: Galileo is the EU's independent satellite navigation system (rival to US GPS) — a civilian multi-satellite navigation project.
factual_hooks: Galileo: EU's civilian satellite navigation system (rival to US GPS)
Provides more accurate positioning than GPS for civilian use
contrast_with: Galileo (EU, civilian) vs GPS (US, military-origin) — civilian control distinction
contemporary_relevance: Competition in GNSS reflects strategic autonomy goals of major powers including India (NavIC).

subject: History
trap_factor: Raja Rammohan Roy also opposed polygamy and is associated with widow issues; Pandita Ramabai wrote about child marriage. Bahubivah is specifically about polygamy.
elimination_hint: Bahubivah (meaning 'polygamy/multiple wives') was written by Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar to campaign against polygamy among Kulin Brahmins.
factual_hooks: Bahubivah: written by Vidyasagar, 1871, against Kulin Brahmin polygamy
Vidyasagar also championed widow remarriage (Widow Remarriage Act 1856)
contrast_with: Vidyasagar's Bahubivah (anti-polygamy) vs his earlier work for widow remarriage

subject: Society
factual_hooks: Feminization of agriculture: women constitute 60-80% of food producers in some states
Male out-migration to cities leaves women as de facto farm managers
contrast_with: Feminization as empowerment vs feminization as burden (without land ownership)
mains_intro: Increasing feminization of agriculture in India reflects a complex interplay of economic distress, male migration, and evolving gender roles in rural areas.
mains_body_points: Economic forces: farm distress, declining agricultural profitability push men to non-farm work; women fill the void without commensurate land rights
Male out-migration (rural-urban): leaves women as de facto farm managers, especially in states like Uttarakhand, J&K, Andhra Pradesh
MGNREGA: increased women's labor force participation in agriculture-adjacent work
Socio-cultural forces: gradual shift in gender norms; women-led SHGs gaining agency over agricultural decisions
But without land ownership, credit access, or extension services, feminization becomes a burden — 'feminization of poverty' risk
mains_conclusion: Making feminization of agriculture truly empowering requires addressing land rights, credit access, and extension services for women farmers.
mains_interlinkages: GS1: Indian Society and women's role
contemporary_relevance: PM-KISAN and other schemes now increasingly target women farmers; PM Fasal Bima Yojana coverage for women is expanding.

subject: Governance
trap_factor: Dilip Kumar, Raj Kapoor and Dev Anand are all legendary actors — but checking whether all three received Dadasaheb Phalke Award requires verification. Dev Anand did not receive it.
elimination_hint: Dilip Kumar (1994) and Raj Kapoor (1987) received Dadasaheb Phalke Awards but Dev Anand never received it — making Statement 3 false. Only Statement 3 being asked → Option D (3 only if all three received it is wrong).
factual_hooks: Dadasaheb Phalke recipients: Dilip Kumar (1994), Raj Kapoor (1987); Dev Anand did NOT receive it
Answer D (3 only) implies only Dilip Kumar and Raj Kapoor, not Dev Anand — but Q implies all three
contrast_with: Phalke Award (lifetime achievement) vs National Award (annual film achievement)

subject: Polity
trap_factor: Articles 355 and 356 are emergency-related; Article 258 transfers Union powers to states; Article 257 is specifically about states not impeding Union executive power.
elimination_hint: Article 257 of the Constitution: state executive power must be exercised so as not to impede Union executive power — the key federalism compliance provision.
factual_hooks: Article 257: States must not impede Union executive power; Union may give directions to States
Article 356: President's Rule; Article 355: Union's duty to protect states
contrast_with: Art 257 (state must not impede Union) vs Art 258 (Union may delegate powers to states)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: India launches satellites from Sriharikota; INSAT-3E was launched from Kourou, French Guiana (Ariane launch) — candidates may select Seychelles or Mauritius as island launch sites.
elimination_hint: INSAT-3E was launched by Ariane rocket from Kourou, French Guiana (South America) — ISRO's heavy communication satellites used Ariane launch services.
factual_hooks: INSAT-3E (2003): launched from Kourou, French Guiana by Ariane 5 rocket
Heavy communication satellites launched by Ariane from French Guiana
contrast_with: Sriharikota (India's own launch site) vs Kourou (for heavy commercial satellites)

subject: History
trap_factor: Memorizing viceroy chronology is challenging; Hardinge I, Chelmsford and Irwin are easily confused in order.
elimination_hint: Correct chronological order: Curzon (1899-1905) → Hardinge II (1910-16) → Chelmsford (1916-21) → Irwin (1926-31) → Option A: 1-3-2-4.
factual_hooks: Viceroy order: Curzon → Hardinge → Chelmsford → Irwin (1-3-2-4)
Irwin: known for Gandhi-Irwin Pact (1931)
contrast_with: Hardinge I (18th century) vs Hardinge II (early 20th century) — different historical periods

subject: History
trap_factor: The chronological order of reform acts (Minto-Morley 1909, Montagu-Chelmsford 1919) and events (Chauri-Chaura 1922, Dandi March 1930) must be precisely matched.
elimination_hint: Minto-Morley (1909) → Montagu-Chelmsford (1919) → Chauri-Chaura (1922) → Dandi March (1930) = 2-4-1-3 → Option B.
factual_hooks: Chronology: Minto-Morley (1909), Montagu-Chelmsford (1919), Chauri-Chaura (1922), Dandi March (1930)
Minto-Morley introduced separate electorates; Montagu-Chelmsford introduced dyarchy
contrast_with: Minto-Morley (communal electorates) vs Montagu-Chelmsford (dyarchy) — different reform approaches

subject: History
trap_factor: Haryana (1966), 4th General Elections (1967), Meghalaya/Tripura full states (1972), Karnataka (1973) — getting these years right in order is the challenge.
elimination_hint: Haryana (1966) → 4th General Elections (1967) → Meghalaya & Tripura full states (1972) → Mysore renamed Karnataka (1973) = 2-1-4-3 → Option A.
factual_hooks: Haryana: 1966; 4th General Elections: 1967; Meghalaya/Tripura: 1972; Karnataka renamed: 1973
States Reorganization Act 1956 was the base; later reorganizations are distinct
contrast_with: Haryana (Punjab bifurcation, 1966) vs Meghalaya (NE Frontier states, 1972)

subject: Ethics
factual_hooks: Public interest: collective welfare over individual/sectional interests
Civil servants bound by AIS conduct rules to serve public interest impartially
contrast_with: Public interest vs private interest — conflict of interest issues
mains_intro: Public interest refers to the general welfare of society that civil servants are constitutionally and ethically obligated to serve above all personal or sectional considerations.
mains_body_points: Definition: Public interest = collective welfare; encompasses social justice, equity, rule of law, and long-term societal good
Principles: Impartiality, non-partisanship, objectivity, accountability, transparency, commitment to rule of law
Procedures: Following laid-down rules; seeking legal advice for ambiguous situations; disclosing conflicts of interest
Ethical dilemmas: When legal compliance conflicts with public good — civil servant must escalate, use legitimate channels
Institutional mechanisms: RTI, CAG, parliamentary oversight, anti-corruption bodies reinforce public interest obligations
mains_conclusion: Public interest is the north star of civil service — its pursuit demands not just rule-following but proactive ethical judgment in service of society.
contemporary_relevance: Policy decisions during COVID-19 tested civil servants' commitment to public interest over political expediency.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Circular motion involves centripetal acceleration — many candidates confuse centripetal (toward centre) with centrifugal (away from centre) or with tangential acceleration.
elimination_hint: Centripetal acceleration = ω²r directed toward the centre; angular velocity = ω (not x in the question, but used as variable). Option B states 'x²r directed towards the centre' = correct form.
factual_hooks: Centripetal acceleration = ω²r = v²/r (directed toward centre)
Uniform circular motion: speed constant, velocity changes direction → acceleration exists
contrast_with: Centripetal (real, toward centre) vs Centrifugal (pseudo, away from centre)

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: 18-carat gold has 18 parts gold — but 'out of how many total parts' confuses candidates between 24 (pure gold standard) and other fractions.
elimination_hint: 24-carat = pure gold. 18-carat = 18 parts gold + 6 parts other metals (out of 24 total) → Option C.
factual_hooks: 18-carat gold: 18 parts gold + 6 parts other metals (out of 24 total)
24-carat = pure gold; 22-carat = 22 parts gold + 2 other
contrast_with: 18-carat (75% pure) vs 22-carat (91.6% pure) — common jewellery standards
contemporary_relevance: BIS hallmarking mandatory for gold jewellery in India from 2021.

subject: Science & Technology
trap_factor: Potassium gives violet color (not a common firework color), mercury compounds are toxic and rarely used — strontium (red) and barium (green) are the standard firework colorants.
elimination_hint: Strontium salts: red/crimson color; Barium salts: green color — the classic fireworks chemistry pair.
factual_hooks: Strontium salts → red/crimson in fireworks; Barium salts → green in fireworks
Sodium: yellow; Potassium: violet; Copper: blue in fireworks
contrast_with: Strontium (red) vs Barium (green) — most tested fireworks pair

subject: History
trap_factor: Statement 1: Third Battle of Panipat was between Ahmed Shah Abdali and the Marathas (not Ibrahim Lodi — that was First Battle of Panipat). Statement 2: Tipu Sultan died in Fourth Anglo-Mysore War (not Third). Statement 3 about Mir Jafar is correct.
elimination_hint: Statement 1 is wrong (Abdali defeated Marathas, not Ibrahim Lodi); Statement 2 is wrong (Tipu died in 4th Anglo-Mysore War); Statement 3 correct (Mir Jafar conspired with English at Plassey). Only 3 → Option B.
factual_hooks: Third Panipat (1761): Abdali defeated Marathas (not Ibrahim Lodi — 1st Panipat was Babur vs Ibrahim)
Tipu Sultan died in 4th Anglo-Mysore War (1799), not 3rd (1790-92)
contrast_with: 1st Panipat (Babur vs Ibrahim Lodi) vs 3rd Panipat (Abdali vs Marathas)
